Challenges with ESG Reporting and Transparency
==============================================

Share

[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3a%2f%2fwww.kroll.com%2fen%2freports%2ffraud-and-financial-crime-report%2fchallenges-with-esg-reporting-and-transparency "Linkedin")[](mailto:?subject=challenges%20with%20esg%20reporting%20and%20transparency&body=https%3a%2f%2fwww.kroll.com%2fen%2freports%2ffraud-and-financial-crime-report%2fchallenges-with-esg-reporting-and-transparency "Email")

[Download the Report](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/krollllc17bf0-kroll6fee-proda464-0e9b/media/Kroll-Images/PDFs/2023-fraud-and-financial-crime-report.pdf)

Firms must make a focused effort to explain and backstop their environmental, social and governance (ESG) initiatives and provide the steps they have taken or will need to take to achieve their goals. Telling the right ESG story in the current environment requires adequate and accurate disclosures both on performance on ESG-related accounting and activity metrics as well as the underlying methodologies, policies and procedures employed.

A best practice methodology includes conducting materiality assessments and measuring the amount of ESG risk and its impact on valuations and operations in both routine and stressful environments. A thorough materiality assessment is an introspective look into a firm that allows it to accurately form its ESG narrative and identify areas where it might improve or would like to further develop.

Most respondents in our survey appear confident that they are telling the right ESG narrative, either agreeing or strongly agreeing that their ESG/Sustainability story is an accurate representation of their activities and mission.

![Image 2: Organizations who are Confident Their ESG Story is an Accurate Representation of Their Company’s Activities and Mission](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/krollllc17bf0-kroll6fee-proda464-0e9b/media/Kroll-Images/Insights/FFCR-Report/Challenges-ESG-Reporting-Graphics/organizations-that-are-confident.svg)

Given the importance of ESG as a mechanism for disclosing and communicating companies’ journeys and performance on sustainability issues, that should be a promising development. However, our survey also confirmed that most companies still feel challenged by the issues of standardization and data integrity that primarily relate to storytelling. That raises concerns that might belie the accuracy and adequacy of their ESG stories and whether they are focusing on the right factors.

Although survey respondents appear to feel confident in the accuracy of their ESG stories, they also state that they continue to face challenges and issues around methodological standardization and data collection, among other issues.

![Image 3: Across the Globe, Main ESG Challenges are Standardization, Data, Cost and Transparency](https://edge.sitecorecloud.io/krollllc17bf0-kroll6fee-proda464-0e9b/media/Kroll-Images/Insights/FFCR-Report/Challenges-ESG-Reporting-Graphics/standardization-data-costs-transparency.svg)

In other words, despite the arguably clearer picture in the current environment, firms continue to struggle with the underlying methodologies and data that enable adequate disclosures of the ESG story. Perhaps that is partly a function of legacy issues from the past decade’s confusion and uncertainty. However, it may also be influenced by heightened temptations to meet “middle-of-the-pack” ESG disclosures and practices and avoid standing out, but while minimizing anticipated costs associated with complying and aligning with current norms. Companies may find it easier to embrace and disclose an unreflective slew of ESG factors and metrics with limited relevance, just to quickly appease certain internal and external stakeholders or align with competitors. However, that approach to ESG is an increasingly risky proposition; current improvements around standardization and heightened scrutiny also means the risks and potential consequences of improper ESG disclosures have also meaningfully increased. Technology is improving the ability to ask standardized questions and gather responses across a portfolio of assets. This enables the highlighting of outliers and risks against a benchmarked view. Technology is not only enabling easier collection of data, but also the continuous assessment of indicators to allow for real-time status of risks.

Green Crimes
------------

Environmental crimes are rarely spoken of within the scope of financial crimes, despite the effects being just as damaging as more commonly recognized forms such as fraud. Until recently, environmental crime was seen as a lower-risk activity for criminal networks, as governments across the world placed priority on tackling drugs, counterfeiting and human trafficking. Across many countries, light sanctions for environmental crimes alongside limited efforts to follow and remove the profits, made participating in such activities a lucrative source of income for criminals. A few examples of green crimes that firms need to be aware of include illegal logging/ trade, trafficking of protected species of animal and plant wildlife, smuggling precious metals and even waste management.

Currently, it is extremely difficult for firms to detect potential environmental crimes as there is no specific criteria of what to look out for or a blacklist of companies that are known to engage in illegal activities related to environmental crimes. However, there are key indicators that should ring alarm bells for firms about the legitimacy of a business and the potential for green crime activity. The main aspect to look out for are potential major inconsistencies, (for example, boards with no experience in the industry in question or companies that hold significantly higher profit margins than others within the sector). This requires an element of proactivity and good industry knowledge to apply in each individual case. Proper due diligence is the best way to identify potential risks associated with green crimes or other ESG-related activity.

The Consequences of Getting ESG Wrong
-------------------------------------

At a minimum, firms that fail to adopt proper ESG practices are likely to misallocate resources and focus on factors of less importance, undermining the point of ESG and its focus on real issues, including the enterprise’s social license to operate, the use of common capitals (or natural public resources) and the negative externalities created by its operations.

Firms that do not adopt current recommendations substantially increase the likelihood of greenwashing, fraud and abuse, either knowingly or unknowingly. Failing to adopt best practices and proper policies and procedures creates conditions for unintentional misstatements and errors. Failure to do so can initiate problematic situations involving outright fraud and criminal malfeasance, both by the organization itself and opportunistic third parties. For company insiders, incentives are extremely high to make statements that suggest excellence in ESG metrics or activities around the level of adoption of policies and procedures.

The Overlooked G in ESG
-----------------------

The governance pillar (G) is sometimes overlooked in the context of ESG programs when compared to its environmental (E) and social (S) counterparts, yet it is the fundamental basis of any ESG compliance program and the initial path to develop and implement any ESG, impact or sustainable investment strategies and priorities for a company. This means defensible, thorough and efficient controls both on the source of data and its analysis. Transparency and discipline also allow stakeholders to understand and balance the business decisions and trade-offs associated with a comprehensive ESG program. It is, in short, the common thread that weaves together all ESG efforts.

Governance is an essential component of managing ESG risks well. The G pillar includes factors such as independence of the board, shareholder rights, executive compensation, risk and control procedures, operational due diligence, anti-competitive practices, business ethics, fraud and respect for the law and regulations. Instances of weak governance invite shareholder litigation and regulatory action.

ESG and Data Standardization
----------------------------

By 2021, 86% of S&P 500 firms regularly issued some kind of ESG-related report, up from 35% of publicly traded companies in 2010, according to the Harvard Business School article. There is no one-size-fits-all solution to a company’s ESG program and although there has been some regulatory guidance and stakeholder pressure, standardization is still lacking. Certain jurisdictions will have different views of ESG and how critical it is to their business’ success.

Regulatory requirements and client, investor and consumer expectations will influence the ESG-related data that is collected. With a variety of ESG reporting frameworks and standards, many organizations are faced with the challenges of aggregating data from their suppliers or portfolio companies or even at their own corporate level. Adding to confusion is the lack of or limited data that companies have access to or are able to aggregate for their own reporting purposes. There is also a lack of verifiable and consistent data. Most firms have limited in-house resources, which may lead to improper and/or inadequate internal controls of their ESG programs. Solutions that bring ESG expertise in-house can be costly. And as with any regulatory framework, it may take time to bring staff up to speed with ESG regulation and reporting expectations. Technology solutions can be beneficial in helping companies standardize their ESG data aggregation, but many providers tend to simply aggregate data without providing meaningful reporting and benchmarking.

According to our survey results, 61% of respondents cited a lack of standardization as a key ESG challenge and 61% cited limited data as their key challenge. Firms concerned about litigation and enforcement risk, particularly in jurisdictions where the regulatory framework is not clear, should ensure that any limitations and weaknesses in data and disclosures are themselves fully understood and disclosed, including any applicable data gaps and methodological limitations. In-house or external counsel should review any related disclosures prior to their release to ensure alignment with applicable local disclosure rules and regulations.

ESG Risks and Rewards
---------------------

As discussed above, transparency is often an effective balm for organizations working with murky data. Delivering on stakeholder expectations while working with uncertain data can be difficult, but truthfulness about the uncertainty, along with a strategy to improve it, is a better outcome than being accused of greenwashing.

Many global regulators are focused on ensuring that firms are adhering to their ESG commitments by providing proper disclosure and reporting to investors. In short, a firm can’t simply state its commitment to ESG without being prepared to provide evidence of adhering to those commitments. Documented compliance programs, data and an understanding of the data’s lineage, and transparency about the data’s limits will be required to respond to client and regulator inquiries.

Successful firms, and those that face the lowest risk, are dispassionately introspective while assessing themselves. They take due diligence, internal education and risk management seriously.

--- SOURCE 2: The 5 Main Challenges of ESG Reporting and Best Practices ---
URL: https://ecoactivetech.com/the-5-main-challenges-of-esg-reporting-and-best-practices/

SUMMARY:
While ESG reporting presents significant opportunities for businesses—such as enhancing brand reputation, improving stakeholder trust, and attracting sustainable investments—it also comes with complex challenges that companies must overcome to ensure accuracy, compliance, and strategic alignment. This absence of a universal ESG reporting framework creates confusion for companies trying to align their sustainability disclosures with multiple reporting guidelines and regulations. As Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting becomes a key requirement for businesses, regulatory bodies across the globe are tightening ESG disclosure rules to ensure transparency, accountability, and risk management. Unlike voluntary sustainability reporting in the past, ESG regulations are now mandatory in many regions, compelling businesses to disclose climate risks, social impact, and corporate governance practices in financial filings.

The 5 Main Challenges of ESG Reporting and Best Practices
=========================================================

February 25, 2025

[](https://ecoactivetech.com/the-5-main-challenges-of-esg-reporting-and-best-practices/#)

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting has become a critical aspect of corporate transparency, enabling businesses to showcase their commitment to sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical governance. With investors, regulators, and consumers demanding greater transparency, organizations are under increasing pressure to integrate ESG considerations into their corporate strategies and financial disclosures.

While ESG reporting presents significant opportunities for businesses—such as enhancing brand reputation, improving stakeholder trust, and attracting sustainable investments—it also comes with complex challenges that companies must overcome to ensure accuracy, compliance, and strategic alignment.

From navigating multiple ESG frameworks to managing vast amounts of ESG data, organizations must adopt structured approaches and leverage advanced ESG reporting platforms like EcoActive to streamline processes and maintain compliance with evolving global standards.

**Challenge 1: Navigating Multiple ESG Reporting Frameworks**

**The Problem: No Universal Standard for ESG Reporting**

ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) reporting has become an essential part of corporate disclosure, with investors, regulators, and consumers demanding greater transparency in sustainability efforts. However, unlike financial reporting—which follows standardized frameworks like Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) or International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)—ESG reporting lacks a single global standard.

This absence of a universal ESG reporting framework creates confusion for companies trying to align their sustainability disclosures with multiple reporting guidelines and regulations. Businesses, especially multinational corporations (MNCs), must comply with various ESG reporting frameworks depending on their industry, region, and investor requirements.

**Why Does the Lack of a Universal ESG Standard Matter?**

**Regulatory Complexity: Different countries and governing bodies impose varying ESG disclosure rules, making compliance challenging and resource-intensive.**

**Inconsistent ESG Data Collection: Companies may collect and report sustainability data using different methodologies, leading to discrepancies in ESG performance evaluations.**

**Stakeholder Confusion: Investors, customers, and regulators struggle to compare ESG reports across companies when multiple frameworks use different metrics and reporting standards.**

**Risk of Greenwashing: Without a single standard, some companies exaggerate or misrepresent their sustainability efforts, leading to reputational risks and potential regulatory penalties.**

**Overview of Key ESG Reporting Frameworks**

Organizations looking to develop an effective ESG reporting strategy must navigate multiple frameworks that cater to different aspects of sustainability disclosures. Below are the most widely recognized ESG reporting frameworks used globally:

1. **Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)**

**Best for: Companies seeking broad sustainability reporting across industries.**

GRI is the most widely adopted ESG reporting standard, designed to help businesses communicate their sustainability impacts on the economy, environment, and society.

Provides comprehensive ESG disclosure guidelines covering carbon emissions, labor rights, anti-corruption measures, and more.

Used by over 10,000 organizations worldwide, including Fortune 500 companies and government agencies.

2. **Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)**

**Best for: Investors and businesses looking for industry-specific ESG performance metrics.**

SASB focuses on financial materiality by identifying ESG factors that impact a company’s financial performance.

 Industry-specific standards cover 77 industries, making it a preferred framework for investors assessing ESG risks and opportunities.

3. **Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)**

**Best for: Companies required to disclose climate-related financial risks.**

Established by the Financial Stability Board (FSB), TCFD focuses on climate risk disclosure to help businesses and investors understand the financial impact of climate change.

Increasingly becoming mandatory in the U.K., EU, and parts of Asia.

4. **International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB)**

**Best for: Companies looking for a unified global ESG reporting standard.**

Launched by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation, ISSB aims to standardize ESG reporting globally.

Expected to harmonize ESG disclosures by integrating elements from GRI, SASB, and TCFD into a single reporting framework.

5. **EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)**

**Best for: Businesses operating in Europe or with European investors.**

The CSRD replaces the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and expands ESG disclosure requirements for companies operating in the European Union (EU).

Introduces the concept of double materiality, meaning companies must report both financial risks and broader social/environmental impacts.

Over 50,000 companies will be required to comply, making it one of the most influential ESG regulations worldwide.

 6. **SEC Climate Disclosure Rule (U.S.)**

**Best for: Publicly traded companies in the United States.**

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is introducing mandatory climate risk disclosures, requiring companies to report greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, climate-related risks, and financial impacts.

Aims to increase transparency and protect investors from ESG-related financial risks.

7. **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)**

CDP is a leading global environmental disclosure system that helps companies, cities, states, and regions measure and manage their impact on climate, water, and forests.

**What it does:**

Collects standardized climate, water, and forest-related data from companies and governments.

Provides insights into corporate sustainability efforts and their alignment with global climate goals.

Used by investors, policymakers, and businesses to assess environmental risks and opportunities.

**Why it matters:**

Over 24,800+ companies and 1,000+ cities report through CDP, covering 66% of global market capitalization.

 Helps organizations identify climate risks, track emissions, and improve sustainability performance.

 Supports compliance with TCFD, CSRD, and other regulatory frameworks.

**8. Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD)**

TNFD is a nature-focused ESG framework designed to help companies identify and report risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems—just as TCFD does for climate risks.

**What it does:**

Provides a structured approach to assessing, managing, and disclosing nature-related financial risks.

 Encourages companies to consider deforestation, biodiversity loss, and ecosystem degradation in financial decision-making.

Aligns with TCFD, ensuring consistency in climate and nature-based reporting.

**Why it matters:**

Helps businesses shift financial flows away from activities that harm biodiversity and toward nature-positive investments.

Supports the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) and global sustainability goals.

Strengthens risk assessment for industries dependent on natural resources (e.g., agriculture, energy, mining, and finance).

9. **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**

The 17 SDGs are a global blueprint for economic, environmental, and social sustainability, adopted by the United Nations (UN) in 2015 to be achieved by 2030.

**What it does:**

Defines clear sustainability targets across climate action, responsible consumption, gender equality, and more.

 Provides a common global framework for governments, businesses, and civil society to align sustainability initiatives.

Helps companies integrate ESG strategies with broader global sustainability goals.

**Why it matters:**

Used as a benchmark for ESG impact measurement and reporting.

Encourages businesses to align operations, supply chains, and investments with sustainable practices.

Investors and stakeholders increasingly assess corporate SDG alignment when making decisions.

**Best Practices: How to Navigate Multiple ESG Frameworks**

**Identify the Most Relevant ESG Standards**

Businesses should analyze their industry, geographic presence, and stakeholder expectations to determine which ESG frameworks best align with their reporting needs.

For example, a financial institution may prioritize SASB, while a manufacturing company may focus on TCFD’s climate-related disclosures.

**Conduct a Gap Analysis**

Perform an internal assessment to compare current ESG reporting efforts against the requirements of relevant frameworks.

Identify areas needing improvement to enhance compliance and reporting quality.

**Utilize ESG Reporting Technology**

EcoActive, an advanced ESG reporting platform, enables organizations to align with multiple ESG frameworks simultaneously by automating data collection, reporting, and compliance tracking.

**Stay Updated on Regulatory Changes**

Governments and regulators frequently update ESG disclosure requirements. Companies must monitor these changes through sources like:

SEC’s ESG reporting regulations (U.S.)

EU’s CSRD and Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR)

UK’s FCA TCFD-aligned ESG guidelines

**Challenge 2: Understanding and Complying with ESG Regulations**

**The Problem: Constantly Changing ESG Regulatory Landscape**

As Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting becomes a key requirement for businesses, regulatory bodies across the globe are tightening ESG disclosure rules to ensure transparency, accountability, and risk management.

For companies, this rapidly evolving regulatory landscape creates significant compliance challenges, requiring them to adapt quickly to new mandates, avoid non-compliance penalties, and maintain stakeholder trust.

Unlike voluntary sustainability reporting in the past, ESG regulations are now mandatory in many regions, compelling businesses to disclose climate risks, social impact, and corporate governance practices in financial filings.

**Why Is ESG Compliance a Challenge for Businesses?**

Constantly Evolving Regulations – ESG laws are frequently updated, requiring companies to track and adapt to new compliance requirements.

Complex ESG Disclosure Requirements – Different countries enforce varied reporting standards, making compliance difficult for multinational corporations (MNCs).

High Cost of ESG Compliance – Businesses must invest in ESG reporting software, regulatory consultants, and internal compliance teams to manage evolving rules.

Risk of Non-Compliance Penalties – Failure to meet ESG disclosure obligations can result in fines, legal actions, reputational damage, and investor withdrawals.

Lack of Standardized Global ESG Regulations – While financial reporting follows GAAP or IFRS, ESG reporting lacks one unified global standard, creating compliance confusion.

**Key ESG Regulatory Developments Worldwide**

Several major regulatory changes have been introduced to enforce ESG reporting compliance. Below are some of the most significant:

1. **SEC’s Climate Disclosure Rule (U.S.) – Effective 2025**

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has introduced a climate-related financial disclosure rule requiring publicly traded companies to report:

Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Scope 1, Scope 2, and in some cases, Scope 3 emissions).

Climate-related financial risks and their impact on business operations.

Board oversight and governance of climate risks.

Failure to comply could result in fines, investor lawsuits, and SEC penalties.

2. **EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) – Effective 2024**

Expands ESG reporting requirements for large companies operating in the European Union.

Introduces double materiality, meaning companies must disclose both:

How ESG risks impact their financial performance.

How their operations affect the environment and society.

Over 50,000 companies must comply, making CSRD one of the most comprehensive ESG regulations worldwide.

3. **UK’s TCFD-aligned ESG Disclosure Rules – Mandatory for Large Businesses**

The Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework is now mandatory for:

Premium-listed companies on the London Stock Exchange.

Banks, insurers, and large private companies.

Companies must disclose climate risks, transition plans, and financial impacts of environmental issues.

4. **ISSB’s Global Sustainability Disclosure Standards (2024)**

The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), created by the IFRS Foundation, aims to establish a global ESG reporting baseline.

Integrates elements from GRI, SASB, and TCFD to create one unified sustainability disclosure standard.

Expected to harmonize ESG reporting across multiple regions.

**The Impact of ESG Regulations on Businesses**

**For Publicly Traded Companies: Must comply with SEC, CSRD, or ISSB disclosure rules to maintain investor confidence and prevent stock price drops due to ESG non-compliance.**

**For Private Companies: Even non-public businesses are now being asked by investors and supply chain partners to disclose ESG risks to maintain business relationships.**

**For Multinational Corporations: Companies with global operations must align with multiple ESG laws, requiring extensive compliance tracking.**

**For SMEs (Small and Medium Enterprises): While direct ESG mandates may not apply, large corporations require ESG compliance from suppliers, affecting business opportunities.**

**Best Practices: How to Ensure ESG Compliance**

To successfully navigate the complex ESG regulatory landscape, businesses must adopt a structured approach to compliance.

**1. Develop an ESG Compliance Team**

Assign dedicated ESG professionals to monitor regulatory updates and reporting obligations.

Engage legal experts and sustainability officers to ensure full compliance.

Conduct regular internal audits to assess ESG reporting accuracy.

**2. Leverage ESG Compliance Technology**

Use ESG reporting platforms like EcoActive to:

Track evolving ESG regulations across different jurisdictions.

Automate compliance reporting to reduce manual workload.

Integrate ESG data from different departments into one standardized report.

**3. Engage with Regulatory Bodies**

Proactively communicate with SEC, EU regulators, and ESG standard-setting organizations.

Participate in public consultations and industry discussions to stay ahead of policy changes.

**4. Provide ESG Compliance Training**

Train internal teams on new ESG disclosure requirements.

Educate executives, board members, and finance teams on the business impact of ESG compliance.

Conduct quarterly ESG workshops to keep employees updated on regulatory changes.

**5. Integrate ESG Compliance into Business Strategy**

Align sustainability goals with corporate risk management and financial reporting.

Use Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to measure ESG compliance progress.

Ensure board-level oversight on ESG strategy and regulatory obligations.

**The Cost of ESG Non-Compliance**

Failing to comply with ESG regulations can result in serious legal and financial consequences, including:

**Regulatory Fines & Penalties – The SEC, EU regulators, and UK authorities impose heavy fines for non-compliance.*

# The Challenges of ESG Reporting: Navigating the Complexity of EU CSRD

![Michael Rasmussen](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/17180ce4c4c5efaa1958eeb9dc5ca319a0578e35dbe2e1125275cf1dc3e99ca0?s=96&d=mm&r=g)
![](https://i0.wp.com/grc2020.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/ff5e4d07-5264-4d0e-bb76-fdfb64e624a0-e1739457265538.webp?fit=1305%2C735&ssl=1)

While the USA is going in different directions, and the EU considers streamlining and integrating requirements later this month, the global landscape of Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting has fundamentally changed with the European Union’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (EU CSRD) first wave of corporate reports being published in 2025. Last week was intense and enlightening in my journeys across Europe, engaging with nearly **60 organizations** across multiple ESG and CSRD discussions.

The journey toward effective ESG reporting is complex, costly, and evolving—but those who embrace it with the right mindset will find not just compliance, but a strategic advantage. **The question is no longer if ESG will shape business operations, but how organizations will rise to the challenge.**

But there are challenges . . . unlike traditional financial reporting, which historically required around 200 data points, ESG reporting under CSRD necessitates over **1,100 data points**, and that number is growing exponentially as companies consider complexities across subsidiaries, divisions, locations, and third-party relationships. This shift is not just a European challenge—CSRD has global implications, impacting **approximately 50,000 companies worldwide**, including non-EU firms with significant operations in Europe.

One of the most pressing challenges of EU CSRD is the requirement for **third-party assurance** on ESG reports. Organizations are already experiencing a **one-third increase in audit fees** due to limited assurance requirements, and these costs will escalate significantly once **reasonable assurance** becomes mandatory. Unlike traditional audits, ESG assurance involves validating complex, qualitative, and often subjective data points, adding further strain on internal resources.

## Two Approaches: Strategic Advantage vs. Checkbox Compliance

One striking observation from my recent workshops across **London, Utrecht, and Stockholm** is the variation in how companies structure ESG ownership. Some firms have designated ESG controllers or sustainability officers, while others distribute ESG responsibilities across **finance, compliance, risk management, legal, audit, and internal control teams**. In certain cases, ESG leaders report directly to the **Board or CEO**, underscoring its strategic significance, while in others, ESG remains a compliance function buried within operational silos.

Among the organizations I engaged with, there was a clear divide in approach:

### The ESG & EU CSRD Insomnia: What Keeps Organizations Awake at Night

During my workshops in **Utrecht and Stockholm**, I facilitated discussions on what keeps organizations up at night regarding ESG and CSRD compliance. Below are the top concerns voiced:

#### **Regulatory & Compliance Challenges**

#### **Data Challenges**

#### **Financial & Resource Constraints**

#### **Strategic and Cultural Implications**

### Where Do Organizations Go From Here?

With the first **CSRD-aligned reports** already being released, it is evident that ESG reporting is more than a regulatory requirement—it is a **fundamental shift in how businesses operate and disclose their impact**. Leading companies are integrating ESG into their core business strategy, governance frameworks, and risk management processes. Those that take a **checkbox approach** risk increased costs, reputational damage, and **regulatory scrutiny**.

As ESG and EU CSRD continue to evolve, organizations must focus on **smarter, data-driven approaches** that align ESG reporting with broader business objectives. Whether through automation, AI-powered compliance tools, or **integrated risk and compliance (GRC) solutions**, the key to ESG success lies in **principled performance rather than reactive compliance**.

In spring 2022, as part of a broader study of cost and managerial accounting information use by German corporations, the authors confidentially interviewed 13 German accounting executives and asked about their companies’ sustainability reporting activities. These executives’ responses, as conveyed and discussed in this article, should provide insight for CPAs employed at U.S.-based companies and audit firms, as sustainability/ESG reporting requirements increase in the United States. To comply with the German Sustainability Code, a company sustainability report must disclose information across 20 sustainability reporting areas: strategy, materiality, objectives, depth of value chain, responsibility, rules and processes, control, incentive schemes, stakeholder engagement, innovation and product management, usage of natural resources, resource management, climate-relevant emissions, employee rights, equal opportunities, qualifications, human rights, corporate citizenship, political influence, and conduct that complies with the law and policy.

Sustainability and ESG Reporting Opportunities and Challenges
=============================================================

Insights from Germany
---------------------

![Image 5](https://www.cpajournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/GettyImages-1307291631-1-730x330.jpg)

#### By[Benjamin P. Foster, CPA, CMA](https://www.cpajournal.com/author/bfoster/ "Posts by Benjamin P. Foster, CPA, CMA") and [Roland Alter, PhD](https://www.cpajournal.com/author/rolandalter/ "Posts by Roland Alter, PhD")

[Featured](https://www.cpajournal.com/category/featured/),[July/August 2024](https://www.cpajournal.com/category/magazine/july-august-2024/),[Sustainability](https://www.cpajournal.com/category/sustainability/)|

January 2025

[Get Copyright Permission](http://www.copyright.com/openurl.do?&issn=0732-8435&WT.mc.id=New%20York%20State%20Society%20of%20Certified%20Public%20Accountants)

Sustainability and environmental, social, and governance (ESG) reporting have grown in scope and importance for several years. This article discusses opportunities and challenges for the accounting profession through insights gleaned from the current state of ESG reporting in Germany. While the future of mandated sustainability reporting in the United States remains uncertain, the climate-related disclosure measures currently issued by the SEC and enacted in California are evidence of an overall trend.

The SEC’s rule,_The Enhancement and Standardization of Climate-Related Disclosures for Investors_([https://tinyurl.com/4u2az6r9](https://tinyurl.com/4u2az6r9))—which has been stayed after being quickly challenged in court—would require registrants to provide certain climate-related information in their registration statements and annual reports. The rule would require information about a registrant’s climate-related risks that are reasonably likely to have a material impact on its business, results of operations, or financial condition. SEC registrants would also be required to include disclosure of their greenhouse gas emissions and some climate-related financial metrics in their audited financial statements.

In late 2022, the Biden administration proposed the Federal Supplier Climate Risks and Resilience Rule. The rule would require organizations with annual federal contracts greater than $50 million (major contractors) to publicly disclose greenhouse gas emissions and climate-related risks. These contractors would also have to set emissions reduction targets. Organizations with federal contracts between $7.5 and $50 million (significant contractors) would have to publicly disclose somewhat less extensive greenhouse gas emission information (Office of the Federal Chief Sustainability Officer: Council on Environmental Quality,[https://tinyurl.com/yc76ndrd](https://tinyurl.com/yc76ndrd)).

Within Europe, and Germany in particular, sustainability and ESG reporting has received more emphasis than in the United States. In spring 2022, as part of a broader study of cost and managerial accounting information use by German corporations, the authors confidentially interviewed 13 German accounting executives and asked about their companies’ sustainability reporting activities. These executives’ responses, as conveyed and discussed in this article, should provide insight for CPAs employed at U.S.-based companies and audit firms, as sustainability/ESG reporting requirements increase in the United States.

### U.S. Regulations and Increased Interest in Sustainability Reporting

For many years, U.S. corporations have been required to comply with laws and regulations related to their foreign suppliers. For example, section 307 of the Tariff Act of 1930 “prohibits importing any product that was mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part by forced labor, including forced or indentured child labor” (Congressional Research Service, July 26, 2022, “Section 307 and Imports by Forced Labor,”_In Focus_,[https://tinyurl.com/5y4yk8wj](https://tinyurl.com/5y4yk8wj)). After several high-profile scandals, Congress enacted the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) in 1977. The FCPA prohibits U.S. companies from engaging in bribery or other corrupt practices when doing business with foreign officials, foreign governments, or foreign businesses. This law applies to all U.S. companies, regardless of their size or location, and failure to comply can result in hefty fines and legal repercussions. As amended in 1998, the FCPA rules also apply to foreign companies and persons engaging in similar actions within the territory of the United States ([https://tinyurl.com/4s4w369r](https://tinyurl.com/4s4w369r)).

The SEC and federal supplier rules might soon lead to required disclosures related to environmental and supply chain practices of many U.S. companies. Momentum is building for international standards and perhaps reporting requirements for ESG areas. Ratings agencies such as Standard & Poor’s, Morningstar, and Moody’s have developed systems and tools for rating companies’ ESG and sustainability efforts.

### Sustainability and ESG Standards and Reporting in Germany and the European Union

Europe, and Germany in particular, have been world leaders in sustainability and ESG reporting. The European Green Deal set out goals for ambitious widespread efforts to combat climate change and environmental degradation. Goals include reducing chemical pesticide use and risk by 50% by 2030 and reducing net greenhouse gas emissions to 55% of those in 1990 by 2030 ([https://tinyurl.com/5fdpn6dt](https://tinyurl.com/5fdpn6dt)).

The Blue Competence initiative is an example of German leadership in the sustainability area. This initiative for the mechanical engineering industry was launched in Germany in 2009 and expanded to the European level in 2012. Originally, the initiative was focused on improving sustainability and energy efficiency practices in the machine tool industry. In 2018, the initiative was expanded to include ESG concepts. Companies participating in the initiative agree to abide by 12 sustainability principles encompassing the ESG dimensions in its production and business practices (CECIMO,[https://tinyurl.com/mdpa9y5](https://tinyurl.com/mdpa9y5)).

In 2021, the German government adopted the Act on Corporate Due Diligence in Supply Chains. Starting in 2023, companies with 3,000 or more employees are subject to its provisions, and companies with 1,000 or more employees are covered beginning in 2024. Companies covered by the act’s provisions are responsible for identifying risks that direct suppliers are violating human rights or engaging in environmental destruction. The act also covers indirect suppliers, if covered companies obtain “substantiated knowledge” of potential abuses. In these cases, companies must take measures to address the violations and provide evidence of these measures to the German Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control, which can issue fines against companies that violate due diligence obligations. In addition, people and entities suffering human rights violations or from environmental destruction can demand that the office act against associated companies operating in Germany (Business & Human Rights Resource Centre, “German Parliament Passes Mandatory Human Rights Due Diligence Law,” June 16, 2021,[https://tinyurl.com/4tbpv6mz](https://tinyurl.com/4tbpv6mz)).

In 2021, the IFRS Foundation formed the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) to develop global standards for sustainability information disclosure. In early 2022, the ISSB released drafts of what would become its first two standards,_IFRS S1: General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information_, and _IFRS S2: Climate-related Disclosures_. In July 2022, the AICPA issued generally favorable comment letters on the proposed standards, offering feedback and suggestions (Bryan Strickland, “AICPA & CIMA Comment on ISSB Drafts of Sustainability Disclosures,”_Financial Management_, July 27, 2022,[https://tinyurl.com/mrpxbjhn](https://tinyurl.com/mrpxbjhn)).

On January 1, 2022, the European Union Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities became effective. This taxonomy was integrated into the German Sustainability Code (DNK) which was designed to make company sustainability reporting more uniform and consistent, like financial reporting (EU Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities,[https://tinyurl.com/2r23kkv5](https://tinyurl.com/2r23kkv5); German Sustainability Code,[https://tinyurl.com/ycmsmmfw](https://tinyurl.com/ycmsmmfw)). To comply with the German Sustainability Code, a company sustainability report must disclose information across 20 sustainability reporting areas: strategy, materiality, objectives, depth of value chain, responsibility, rules and processes, control, incentive schemes, stakeholder engagement, innovation and product management, usage of natural resources, resource management, climate-relevant emissions, employee rights, equal opportunities, qualifications, human rights, corporate citizenship, political influence, and conduct that complies with the law and policy.

![Image 6: Industry; Number; Sales, in millions; Employees IT systems services and consulting; 2; Both have > €10,000; Both > 50,000 Leasing services subsidiary of German-based conglomerate; 1; Conglomerate has > €10,000; Conglomerate has > 50,000 Manufacturers/developers of products; 10; From €373 to > €10,000; From 2,200 to > 50,000](https://www.cpajournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/GettyImages-1307291631-1-65x65.jpg)

### Insight 1:

This exhaustive set of sustainability reporting areas in the German Sustainability Code exemplifies the extensive amount of information required for sustainability/ESG reporting and the potential resources necessary to comply with reporting requirements. As such, sustainability reporting presents a huge opportunity for growth for the accounting profession. But the breadth of information desired by sustainability stakeholders and advocates also creates challenges for organizations and accountants.

### German Accounting Executives’ Perceptions of Sustainability Reporting

[_**Exhibit 1**_](https://www.nysscpa.org/news/publications/the-cpa-journal/article-detail?ArticleID=14350#T1)provides information about the industry and size of participants’ employers, which include many very large organizations and some medium-sized manufacturers.

#### EXHIBIT 1

Description of Interviewees’ Companies

![Image 8: Industry; Number; Sales, in millions; Employees IT systems services and consulting; 2; Both have > €10,000; Both > 50,000 Leasing services subsidiary of German-based conglomerate; 1; Conglomerate has > €10,000; Conglomerate has > 50,000 Manufacturers/developers of products; 10; From €373 to > €10,000; From 2,200 to > 50,000](https://www.cpajournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/GettyImages-1307291631-1-65x65.jpg)

### Insight 2:

All German accounting executives who participated in the authors’ interviews indicated that sustainability is important to top corporate executives, and not just from a reporting and public relations standpoint. For example, one participant from a manufacturer noted that corporate executives were concerned about issues like noise that might impact people around their facilities. The accounting executive for another manufacturer said, “The company attempts to reduce its CO 2 footprint by local sourcing of items. We now purchase soft drinks for our facilities from a local bottler rather than a large international brand.”

### Insight 3:

All executives interviewed by the authors noted that their companies realize sustainability policies and reporting are very important and challenging issues. Several of the companies track their carbon emissions, with two executives saying that their companies are now carbon emission neutral. As of the time of the survey, the standards on what, and how, to report were not finalized, which presented obvious challenges. Some participants mentioned that they hope the latest version of SAP will provide a system for capturing sustainability information. SAP and other ERP system providers are reportedly working toward that goal.

### Insight 4:

Estimated sustainability costs that do not require direct euro spending are not calculated. Executives were asked if the overall sustainability costs of direct inputs, distribution costs, or general organization activities are evaluated or included in the cost of products or services. All indicated they were working in this area. Currently, only sustainability efforts that require actual euros expended are allocated to products or cost centers. A top financial executive from a large Germany-based conglomerate stated the following:

This is becoming more important. Questions still remain about what is sustainability, what should be measured, and how that should be reported. Within our ERP system we now track only actual euros-out costs. Management believes that looking at initial sustainability costs in euros will save euros in the long run. We do have some environmental KPIs and track our CO 2 emissions.

### Insight 5:

Companies that assist organizations in implementing and operating IT systems face similar issues as their customers and clients. Two accounting executives from consulting/information technology companies stated the following about their own systems:

Sustainability cost information is not integrated into the accounting system. But sustainability is important in all areas of the business. For example, when consultants travel they receive information from the airline or train company for a measure of the required CO 2 emissions. This information may be collected into our information systems, but it’s not collected in the ERP system.

We are taking steps for sustainability. The company tracks several measures as KPIs, like CO 2 emissions and average kilometers per gallon for auto fleet. (We have transitioned from combustible engines to hybrid and other engines.) We also calculate the amount generated/saved by solar panels installed on our property.

### Insight 6:

The German Act on Corporate Due Diligence in Supply Chains may require disclosures regarding the sustainability costs of companies’ overall supply and distribution networks. One executive for a manufacturer said he hoped SAP S/4 would have some system to track that type of information. They now track their own CO 2 emissions and are heading toward tracking those of their suppliers. An executive at another manufacturer noted that they were considering sustainability performance throughout their distribution chain. He believed it would require a lot of money and effort to do that. He saw that regulations/laws, however, would require supply chain due diligence activity as part of ESG activities.

The accounting executive for another manufacturer said:

We try to use ecologically friendly energy sources. We have a goal to reduce our CO 2 footprint 50% by 2030. The company now uses independent freight haulers, but will next compare the impact of those to other potential alternatives. We are also asking about our supplier companies’ CO 2 emissions.

### Insight 7:

Sustainability reporting is still evolving in Germany and the European Union. One accounting executive mentioned that he believed all German companies are interested in trying to make themselves look good on sustainability. A manufacturing company accounting executive mentioned that they would perhaps confer with auditors on what they need to do to obtain a “good” sustainability opinion in order to make sure they do what is necessary to obtain the desired opinion.

The importance of good sustainability ratings was emphasized by several executives who mentioned that being able to classify an investment as “sustainable” can lead to lower interest rates. German banks are pressured to show sustainable lending practices, which leads them to pressure customers on sustainability issues. One executive from a company specializing in high-euro investments stated that, “Lower interest rates are charged on sustainable investments and the future value of assets may change depending upon regulations.” That company is working on sustainability related to the supply chain, but finding it very difficult:

We comply with CO2 emission standards, but how do we estimate emissions by customers for our equipment? Will the customer have to pay a higher price for higher emissions? We have not decided on a strategy to deal with that issue.

Similarly, accounting executives for manufacturers noted customer concerns about issues related to the products they purchase. Customers are very interested in the pollution generated by purchased machines after installation, and are asking about assurance that child labor is not used in their supply chain. One executive particularly mentioned that auto manufacturers asked them about issues related to their supply chain and that they need to find a practical way to answer those questions.

### Examples of Impact on the German Accounting Profession and New Opportunities

The accounting executive interviews reflected the increasing relevance of ESG-related issues and ESG-related reporting for German businesses. This increased relevance and emphasis requires more professional support for accountants. In recognition of this development, the German Institute of Certified Public Accountants (IDW) released guidelines for the certification as Sustainability-Auditor in March 2023 (“Richtlinie des Instituts der Wirtschaftsprüfer in Deutschland e.V. (IDW) zur Anerkennung des Sustainability-Auditor,” IDW, Mar. 1, 2023,[https://tinyurl.com/bdedarp5](https://tinyurl.com/bdedarp5)).

Another indicator of the growing relevance of ESG reporting is gleaned by the accounting job offerings on job portals. Such ESG-related jobs come from a variety of industries, including those considered as “old” economy. The sidebar,_ESG Manager Job Offering, Schuler_, shows the profile of an ESG manager position offered by Schuler, a leading manufacturer of heavy-duty presses for the automotive industry. The characteristics required for this position include a deep knowledge of ESG reporting standards and the related reporting processes. The skills necessary for an ESG nanager overlap greatly with the requirements of the Sustainability-Auditor professional certification initiative of IDW.

### Learning from Early Adopters

Examples from outside the United States illustrate the potential for widely expanded needs for data collection, reporting, and attestation services related to sustainability. This developing market presents immense opportunities for U.S. accounting professionals. Capitalizing on them will require diligence to become informed on sustainability reporting standards and initiatives, become adept at overseeing data collection and reporting methods, and accepting new challenges for their employers and clients.

### ESG Manager Job Offering, Schuler

**YOUR TASKS:**

*   ▪ Support of the ESG data platforms with regard to ESG issues.
*   ▪ Central contact person for ESG/CSR/EU taxonomy issues for Schuler group companies, internal audit, and auditors in the course of internal and external (financial statement) audits
*   ▪ Coordination of group-wide ESG reporting processes, including preparation of sustainability reporting, support in ESG investor communication, and further development of risk and opportunity management for sustainability issues
*   ▪ Monitoring and evaluating regulatory developments in the area of sustainability and deriving recommendations for management

**YOUR PROFILE:**

*   ▪ Successfully completed (technical) university studies in economics, industrial engineering, or a comparable degree
*   ▪ Several years of professional experience at a management consultancy/auditing firm and/or at an industrial/commercial company in a similar function
*   ▪ Expert knowledge of ESG standards/reporting requirements according to GRI, CSRD, ESRS, etc., and interest in sustainability management; additional qualification in the field of CSR (e.g., as a CSR manager) is an advantage
*   ▪ Good MS Office skills
*   ▪ High analytical and conceptual skills, very good self-organization, as well as independent and result-oriented working style
*   ▪ Ability to work in a team and network, as well as very good communication skills
*   ▪ Business fluent in English and German

SUMMARY:
The reason for this is simple: ESG compliance is now a core business requirement, affecting supply chain management, risk assessments, and investment decisions. The EU’s Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) will require large companies to assess and mitigate ESG risks in their supply chains. The EU has the most comprehensive and enforceable ESG compliance framework, covering corporate disclosures, supply chain due diligence, and sustainable finance. *   **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD):** Requires detailed ESG disclosures from large EU and non-EU companies operating within the bloc. *   **Canada’s Bill S-211** requires companies to report on forced labor risks in supply chains, similar to the UK Modern Slavery Act. Applies to public and private businesses meeting revenue and operational thresholds.

ESG Compliance: Regulations, Risks, and Strategies for Businesses
=================================================================

[Back to Glossary Page](https://ecovadis.com/glossary/)

ESG compliance is no longer just a regulatory requirement – it’s a business imperative. Governments worldwide are tightening environmental, social, and governance regulations, investors are scrutinizing ESG performance, and consumers expect businesses to operate responsibly. Companies that fail to comply face legal penalties, supply chain disruptions, and loss of market access.

This guide breaks down what ESG compliance means, why it matters, and how businesses can meet evolving requirements. It covers key regulations across major markets, challenges companies face in implementation, and best practices for integrating ESG into corporate strategy.

What is ESG Compliance?
-----------------------

ESG compliance means following laws, regulations, and industry standards that cover environmental impact, social responsibility, and corporate governance. It requires businesses to track and report on areas like carbon emissions, labor rights, anti-bribery policies, and board oversight. It can be split into the three components that make up ESG:

*   **Environmental compliance:** Meeting emission reduction targets, following waste disposal laws, and improving energy efficiency. Many companies must report scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions under frameworks like the SEC Climate Disclosure Rule or the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).
*   **Social compliance:** Involves labor laws, supply chain due diligence, and human rights protections. Regulations like Germany’s Supply Chain Act (LkSG) and Canada’s Modern Slavery Act (Bill S-211) require businesses to monitor and prevent forced labor in their supply chains.
*   **Governance compliance:** Financial transparency, anti-corruption efforts, and board diversity. Companies must follow rules like the UK Bribery Act or the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) to prevent fraud and unethical business practices.

In addition to complying with laws and regulations, many organizations voluntarily comply with frameworks like GRI, SASB, and ISSB to meet investor expectations and improve sustainability reporting. The reason for this is simple: ESG compliance is now a core business requirement, affecting supply chain management, risk assessments, and investment decisions. It’s therefore good practice to tick as many boxes as possible.

Why ESG Compliance Matters
--------------------------

Indeed, as much as ESG compliance might feel like a box-ticking exercise, it’s about far more than simply meeting specified requirements and calling it a day. Scrutiny of ESG issues, not just from regulators but also consumers and business partners, is intensifying.

Organizations must be ready to meet strict ESG requirements or face financial penalties, legal action, and reputational damage. Investors and customers now demand transparency, and those that fall behind risk losing access to capital and market opportunities.

### Legal and Regulatory Risk Mitigation

Governments worldwide are expanding ESG compliance laws, shifting from voluntary guidelines to legally enforceable requirements. The EU’s Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) will require large companies to assess and mitigate ESG risks in their supply chains. Germany’s Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG) fines non-compliant companies up to 2% of global turnover, while the UK Modern Slavery Act mandates annual statements on human rights risks.

Environmental compliance is also tightening. The EU’s [Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism-cbam/) puts a price on carbon, forcing companies in high-emission industries to cut emissions or pay significant costs. California’s [SB 253](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/california-climate-corporate-data-accountability-act-sb253/) will require companies with over $1 billion in revenue to report scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions, data that many businesses still struggle to collect and verify.

Failure to comply with these regulations leads to more than just fines. Lawsuits, supply chain disruptions, and investor divestment are real consequences for businesses that lack ESG oversight. Companies operating across multiple jurisdictions must navigate overlapping and sometimes conflicting regulatory frameworks, requiring a structured, proactive compliance strategy.

### Investor and Consumer Expectations

ESG performance is now a financial issue. Investment funds managing over $40 trillion in assets integrate ESG factors into decision-making, and failing to disclose ESG risks can limit a company’s access to financing. The Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR) classifies investment funds based on ESG criteria, influencing how capital is allocated. Companies with weak ESG ratings may struggle to secure loans, attract investors, or issue green bonds.

Consumer expectations are also shifting. 78% of global consumers prefer sustainable products, and major retailers increasingly require suppliers to meet ESG standards. Companies in industries like fashion, electronics, and food must demonstrate traceability in their supply chains, comply with deforestation-free sourcing rules, and prove ethical labor practices to avoid losing contracts.

Corporate buyers are also under pressure. Many firms require their suppliers to complete ESG assessments before entering into procurement agreements. Suppliers with poor ESG scores may be excluded from supply chains entirely.

### Competitive Advantage and Reputation Management

Regulatory and stakeholder pressures aside, ESG compliance offers a strategic advantage. Companies with strong ESG policies secure better supply chain relationships, lower financing costs, and improved risk management.

Greenwashing – making misleading sustainability claims – is under heavy scrutiny. The EU Green Claims Directive will impose strict penalties on businesses that can’t substantiate their environmental claims. Regulatory bodies in the US (SEC), UK (CMA), and Australia (ASIC) are investigating companies for deceptive ESG reporting.

Beyond compliance, ESG programs drive operational efficiencies. Companies that optimize energy use, reduce waste, or transition to circular economy models cut costs and improve resilience against resource shortages. ESG-aligned businesses are also better positioned to win government contracts, as many public procurement policies now require suppliers to meet sustainability criteria.

Key ESG Regulations Around the World
------------------------------------

Regulations vary dramatically by region, but they all point to a clear trend: governments are imposing stricter reporting and due diligence requirements. Organizations with international operations must comply with overlapping and sometimes conflicting rules, making global ESG compliance a strategic but necessary challenge to overcome.

### ESG Compliance in the European Union

The EU has the most comprehensive and enforceable ESG compliance framework, covering corporate disclosures, supply chain due diligence, and sustainable finance.

*   **[Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/corporate-sustainability-reporting-directive-csrd/):** Requires detailed ESG disclosures from large EU and non-EU companies operating within the bloc. Expands reporting obligations under European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and aligns with ISSB and TCFD frameworks.
*   **[Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-corporate-sustainability-due-diligence-directive-csddd/):** Mandates human rights and environmental due diligence across global supply chains. Large EU firms (and non-EU companies with significant EU revenue) must identify, mitigate, and report on ESG risks in their operations, subsidiaries, and supply chains. Failure to comply can result in fines and liability for damages.
*   **[EU Taxonomy](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-taxonomy/):** Defines what qualifies as a sustainable economic activity, guiding investors and businesses. Companies seeking green financing must align with EU Taxonomy criteria, which determine eligibility for green bonds, loans, and sustainability-linked investments.

Additionally, the [EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-deforestation-regulation-eudr/) bans imports of deforestation-linked commodities like soy, palm oil, coffee, and rubber unless organizations can prove their supply chains are deforestation-free.

### United States ESG Regulations

The US lacks a single federal ESG law, and with the [SEC climate disclosure rule](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/) on hold, several state-level ESG laws are emerging, including New York’s proposed climate disclosure rules and Colorado’s ESG reporting standards for financial institutions.

*   **[California SB 253](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/california-climate-corporate-data-accountability-act-sb253/):** The Climate Corporate Data Accountability Act mandates scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions reporting for companies with over $1 billion in revenue operating in California. It’s the strictest climate disclosure law in the US and will likely influence federal regulations.
*   **Dodd-Frank Act (Conflict Minerals Rule):** Requires US-listed companies to disclose sourcing of tin, tungsten, tantalum, and gold (3TG) from conflict-affected regions. A key part of supply chain ESG compliance, non-compliance can lead to penalties and reputational risk.

### UK & Canada ESG Regulations

The UK has moved ahead with mandatory ESG disclosures, particularly in climate risk reporting and sustainable finance. Meanwhile, Canada has introduced measures focusing on forced labor risks.

*   [**UK Modern Slavery Act**](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/uk-modern-slavery-act/) requires companies with annual revenues above £36 million to publish an annual slavery and human trafficking statement. Businesses must assess forced labor risks across supply chains, detail mitigation efforts, and disclose actions to prevent human rights abuses.
*   [**Canada’s Bill S-211**](https://resources.ecovadis.com/blog/canada-s-modern-slavery-legislation-supply-chain-transparency-is-required) requires companies to report on forced labor risks in supply chains, similar to the UK Modern Slavery Act. Applies to public and private businesses meeting revenue and operational thresholds.

Challenges in ESG Compliance Implementation
-------------------------------------------

Complying with ESG regulations and standards is no easy process, not least when you’ve got to meet the requirements of several. Regulations are also evolving fast, and organizations must adapt to new requirements while managing data inconsistencies, resource constraints, and supplier compliance gaps.

### Data Collection and Standardization

Many organizations struggle to collect accurate and verifiable ESG data, especially for scope 3 emissions and supply chain risk assessments. Unlike the scope 1 and 2 categories, which are under an organization’s direct control, Scope 3 emissions depend on external suppliers and partners, making reliable data collection difficult.

The lack of standardized ESG reporting frameworks adds another layer of complexity. Organizations must navigate the CSRD, TCFD, GRI, SASB, and ISSB standards, each with different disclosure requirements. This inconsistency makes data comparisons difficult and increases the risk of non-compliance when reporting across multiple jurisdictions.

### Keeping Up with Regulatory Changes

Governments are tightening ESG laws, introducing mandatory reporting, stricter supply chain due diligence, and financial penalties for non-compliance. Those operating in multiple regions must track regulatory updates in real time to avoid falling behind.

Efforts to harmonize global ESG standards, such as the ISSB’s IFRS S1 & S2, aim to create a single global baseline for sustainability reporting. While this reduces long-term complexity, companies must still adjust their reporting processes to align with these new frameworks. Transitioning to these standards requires investments in data collection systems and training compliance teams to interpret evolving requirements.

### Supply Chain Due Diligence

Most ESG risks occur outside an organization’s direct operations, making supply chain due diligence a major compliance challenge. Identifying ESG risks beyond Tier 1 suppliers can be difficult due to limited visibility into subcontractors and raw material sourcing.

Many suppliers, particularly in emerging markets, lack the resources or expertise to meet ESG compliance standards. Large companies are expected to audit, train, and support suppliers in meeting due diligence requirements, which can be resource-intensive. Non-compliant suppliers pose legal, financial, and reputational risks, and failing to enforce ESG standards in the supply chain can result in penalties under CSDDD or the UK Modern Slavery Act.

Best Practices for ESG Compliance
---------------------------------

ESG compliance requires a structured approach that integrates risk management, data collection, governance, and supplier oversight. Businesses that implement clear policies, leverage technology, and engage suppliers effectively are better positioned to meet regulatory requirements and stakeholder expectations.

### Establishing an ESG Compliance Framework

This begins with identifying material risks and aligning corporate policies with global standards.

*   Materiality assessments help determine which ESG issues pose the greatest financial, operational, and reputational risks. This process ensures that businesses prioritize compliance efforts where they matter most.
*   Organizations should align with recognized ESG reporting frameworks like GRI, TCFD, SASB, and ISSB. These standards provide structured reporting guidelines that meet investor and regulatory expectations.
*   Organizations must embed ESG compliance into corporate strategy, ensuring that executives, legal teams, and procurement managers work together to integrate sustainability into operations.

### Strengthening Governance and Reporting Practices

ESG compliance requires clear accountability and independent verification to ensure credibility and prevent greenwashing risks.

*   Assigning a Chief Sustainability Officer (CSO) or ESG Compliance Lead ensures ESG policies are enforced across departments and compliance efforts are coordinated with financial and legal teams.
*   Third-party ESG audits provide independent verification of sustainability reports, supply chain due diligence, and emissions tracking. Many regulations, including the CSRD and California SB 253, require external assurance for reported data.
*   Board oversight and executive incentives linked to ESG performance help drive accountability. Organizations that tie executive compensation to ESG goals are more likely to implement effective compliance measures.

### Supplier Engagement and Risk Management

As we highlighted earlier, many ESG compliance risks come from supplier operations. This makes supplier engagement an important component of any ESG compliance strategy.

*   Establishing Supplier Codes of Conduct aligned with the CSDDD, UK Modern Slavery Act, and forced labor laws sets clear ESG expectations for vendors. These codes should include minimum labor standards, environmental targets, and ethical sourcing requirements.
*   Companies should train suppliers on ESG compliance, providing capacity-building programs and sustainability workshops to help smaller vendors improve environmental and human rights practices.
*   Supplier ESG performance assessments – including on-site audits, self-assessments, and [third-party evaluations](https://ecovadis.com/solutions/ratings/) – help businesses identify high-risk suppliers and take corrective actions before violations occur.

Managing ESG compliance is complex, but it doesn’t have to slow you down. EcoVadis provides trusted ESG ratings, supplier risk mapping and AI-driven analytics to help you meet global regulations. enriched_points.expanded_content: Companies face confusion due to multiple ESG reporting frameworks like GRI, SASB, and TCFD, complicating compliance and data aggregation across different jurisdictions.
Rising costs for ESG compliance stem from technology investments, third-party audits, and dedicated personnel, straining resources and impacting overall business operations.
Without standardized reporting, companies may exaggerate sustainability efforts, leading to reputational damage and regulatory scrutiny if claims are found to be misleading.
